note: Seed L2 alignment records are citation-level and metadata-only. Full textbook or test content is not included.

data.note: Most difficult JLPT level; official summaries emphasize understanding Japanese used in a broad range of real-life scenes.

data.note: Advanced JLPT level; official summaries place N1/N2 in broad real-life Japanese use.

data.label: JLPT N3
data.note: Bridge level between N4/N5 basic classroom Japanese and N1/N2 advanced real-life Japanese.

data.note: Level alignment only.

curricula.name: BJT Business Japanese Proficiency Test
curricula.license: Official level metadata and citation only; no sample questions or test content.

data.label: BJT J5
data.note: Official level guideline: virtually no ability to communicate in Japanese in business situations.

data.label: BJT J4
data.note: Official level guideline: minimal communication in limited business situations.

data.label: BJT J3
data.note: Official level guideline: some communication in limited business situations.

data.label: BJT J2
data.note: Official level guideline: appropriate communication in a limited range of business situations.

data.label: BJT J1
data.note: Official level guideline: appropriate communication in a wide range of business situations.

data.label: BJT J1+
data.note: Official level guideline: sufficient communication in any business situation.

curricula.name: The Acquisition of Japanese as a Second Language and Processability Theory: A Longitudinal Study of a Naturalistic Child Learner
curricula.license: Citation-level bibliographic metadata only; no thesis text included.

data.label: Child JSL verbal morphosyntax emergence
data.citation: Iwasaki, J. (2006). The Acquisition of Japanese as a Second Language and Processability Theory: A Longitudinal Study of a Naturalistic Child Learner [Doctoral thesis, Edith Cowan University].
data.note: The longitudinal case examines emergence in verbal inflection, V-te V, passive, and causative structures.
data.evidencePopulation: One seven-year-old naturalistic child JSL learner.
data.alignmentLimit: Phenomenon-level citation only; not evidence for adult JLPT placement, taxonomy prerequisites, instructional order, or mastery thresholds.

curricula.name: A Processability Theory (PT)-based Analysis of the Acquisition of Japanese Morphology and Syntax: A Case of an Intensive Adult Learner
curricula.license: Citation-level bibliographic metadata only; no article text included.

data.label: Intensive adult JSL morphosyntax emergence
data.citation: Iwasaki, J. (2013). A Processability Theory (PT)-based Analysis of the Acquisition of Japanese Morphology and Syntax: A Case of an Intensive Adult Learner. Second Language, 12, 21-42.
data.note: The longitudinal case examines emergence orders for verbal affixes, V-te V, passive, causative, benefactive, and canonical and non-canonical sentence structures.
data.evidencePopulation: One 33-year-old beginner receiving approximately 900 hours of intensive one-to-one instruction.
data.alignmentLimit: Phenomenon-level citation only; a single intensive case does not validate a general adult sequence, JLPT placement, taxonomy prerequisites, or mastery thresholds.

curricula.name: The Acquisition of Verbal Morphology in Japanese as a Second Language (JSL) by a Child: Emergence and Accuracy
curricula.license: Citation-level bibliographic metadata only; no article text included.

data.label: Child JSL verbal morphology: emergence and accuracy
data.citation: Iwasaki, J., & Oliver, R. (2014). The Acquisition of Verbal Morphology in Japanese as a Second Language (JSL) by a Child: Emergence and Accuracy. Studies in Language Sciences, 13, 70-95.
data.note: The longitudinal case treats first emergence and target-like rule application as distinct observations.
data.evidencePopulation: One seven-year-old naturalistic child JSL learner observed longitudinally.
data.alignmentLimit: Supports the observational distinction only. The taxonomy's learning-log procedure is a project-authored, processability-informed pedagogical inference, not a validated intervention or diagnostic.

curricula.name: Describing the Acquisition of the Passive Voice by a Child Learner of Japanese as a Second Language from a Processability Theory Perspective
curricula.license: Citation-level bibliographic metadata only; no article text included.

data.label: Child JSL passive-voice development
data.citation: Iwasaki, J., & Oliver, R. (2018). Describing the Acquisition of the Passive Voice by a Child Learner of Japanese as a Second Language from a Processability Theory Perspective. International Journal of Applied Linguistics and English Literature, 7(5), 247-259.
data.note: The longitudinal case focuses on development of passive voice and non-canonical argument-function mapping.
data.evidencePopulation: One naturalistic child JSL learner with English as the first language.
data.alignmentLimit: Phenomenon-level citation only; not evidence for adult N3 placement, taxonomy prerequisites, instructional order, or mastery thresholds.

curricula.name: Acquiring Content Questions in Japanese: The Case of a Sequential English-Japanese Bilingual Child
curricula.license: Citation-level bibliographic metadata only; no article text included.

data.label: Sequential bilingual child Japanese content-question development
data.citation: Kawaguchi, S., & Iwasaki, J. (2021). Acquiring Content Questions in Japanese: The Case of a Sequential English-Japanese Bilingual Child. The International Journal of Early Childhood Learning, 28(1), 39-60.
data.note: The study examines emerging Japanese content interrogatives in a sequential English-Japanese bilingual child.
data.evidencePopulation: One sequential English-Japanese bilingual child observed longitudinally.
data.alignmentLimit: Phenomenon-level citation only; not evidence for adult A1 or JLPT N5 placement, taxonomy prerequisites, instructional order, or mastery thresholds.
